en.titleWrongAnswer: You were caught! But don't worry.
en.bodyPlayAgainWarning: You can play levels again if you want to earn 3 stars!
en.bodyInertiaTutorial_sand: Press the arrow keys to move.
en.c22c8341: All right, I’m all set up!
en.ae894597: Fire Academy was hard but I’m excited for my first day as a firefighter!
en.f52f6b7d: Hey there, welcome! I see you’re ready to start!
en.817a2481: Oh no! Seems like a fire broke out in the city! Quick, take this to try to stop it!
en.19dfe2bf: It's an experimental water and foam jet!
en.41be57b7: What did you notice while putting out the fires?
en.41be57b7_answer_0: One substance seemed to put out fires faster
en.41be57b7_answer_1: Both substances take the same time to put out fires
en.41be57b7_answer_2: None of the substances managed to put out any fires
en.da2c2123: Oh wow, you really got them good! I got to be honest, I thought you would be toast by now.
en.62adbf46: But I noticed something curious! Do you know what it was?
en.45e33f60: But I noticed something curious! Do you know what it was
en.5a77791f: That’s right, it did seem like one substance was better than the other
en.d4908fc0: Hum… that doesn’t seem right, I saw one substance putting out fires faster
en.19edc2e5: But we need to be sure! And I know just the way, with the Scientific Method!
en.5cfd2748: The Scientific Method is a way to check if something is true or not, through a set of steps.
en.181f8b40: The Scientific Method starts with a question, which is what we just had. We asked ourselves what we noticed putting the fire out.
en.b263d493: Now, the next step is a hypothesis, which basically is a guess about the answer to a scientific question.
en.d5e5c154: Sometimes scientists actually have a hypothesis before an experiment, so I think we should try that too!
en.b6e5ecfe: What hypothesis do you think makes more sense?
en.b6e5ecfe_answer_0: Foam puts out fires faster than water
en.b6e5ecfe_answer_1: Water puts out fires faster than foam
en.b6e5ecfe_answer_2: The fire disappears faster on its own
en.8f35b057:
Conclusion:
Foam puts out fires faster than water
en.12158163:
Hypothesis:
Foam puts out fires faster than water
en.f7a18afe:
Hypothesis:
Water puts out fires faster than foam
en.16acdbc0:
Hypothesis:
The fire disappears faster on its own
en.f2ba4dba: We shall put it to the test then, we’ll see if you’re right. Our plan is to use both and check! Let’s carry that out next time-
en.bfabc5e1: Well, that’s convenient…although it’s still a fire. Come on rookie, go get them! And don’t forget to observe!
en.e0c2807f: Well, that’s convenient…although it’s still a fire. Come on rookie, go get them! And don’t forget to observe!
en.90e97584: Very good! Seems like we are going in the right direction. Now we need to find a way to test our question.
en.eb083494: A testable question is one you can answer with observations in a Experiment, the next step of the Scientific Method.
en.562e2356: But when you’re making an experiment, it’s important to check that the question is actually testable.
en.d8c6103c: Something else that is really important is to understand the variables. They are basically a part of the experiment that is observed, changed, or both.
en.6e5553f9: With all that said, let’s prepare for the experiment! What do you think we will need?
en.6a0cbe1d: Start the second experiment and observe again.
en.08a68c2b: Yeah, sounds like you thought of everything! I actually know of a place where you can get all of that-
en.51b0c70a: This is getting ridiculous! The place where the materials are is on fire too! You know what to do, put that fire out and get the materials!
en.8dc96888: Look at that! You did it again! And got all the materials to boot!
en.f597a19b: Okay, so we need to decide what we will observe in this experiment. I believe that what we need to observe is which material puts out fire faster the the other.
en.8f590586: Data that can be measured precisely, like units of distance or time, is a kind of quantitative data.
en.552f941a: Data that can’t be measured precisely but can be described is called Qualitative Data, like observing if something is faster than some other thing.
en.86984d3c: That being said, we should start preparing for the trials.
en.67486d7e: That's right! I also noticed that foam was definitely faster during this trial!
en.9c14138c: In the conclusion, we will compare our prediction during the investigation to the results we had.
en.6a7f0924: The team will love to know this! We will be sure to use that knowledge next time-
en.069eb7a1: Oh, this is it! Now that we know foam is better, I’ll give you all the foam we got! Go show them who’s boss!
en.39bc8762: They are delicious! But not what we need...
en.7e55592e: Oh no that hammer is dangerous! I don't think we need it right now.
en.7b6e4546: Maybe if we had time to make a salad...but not for the experiment.
en.6b89864a: That's my favorite mug! But it's not very usefull right now
en.1e56aaa8: Click on the boxes to start a fire.
en.6d57c4e2: Correct! The stopwatch will be used to time the experiment.
en.4e2a1921: Water puts out fires faster than foam
en.233d4626: Quantitative Data = Can be counted or measured
en.3c502ff9: Qualitative Data = Can NOT be counted or measured
en.823a09d6: Well, since it’s your first day you won't need to do much, just get used to everything and relax-
en.9dfaf8d6: But just to be sure, let's make one more trial! This time with more boxes and A LOT MORE FIRE!!!
en.10fcd887: Well, I think we’re ready for the last step of the Scientific Method, the conclusion!
en.loadingHint1: Your backpack targets fires automatically when you get close!
en.loadingHint2: Don't get too close to the fire, you might hurt yourself.
en.loadingHint3: If an enemy has an item you'll get it automatically when you defeat it.
en.loadingHint4: Sir Francis Bacon, is considered the father of the Scientific Method. But many other important people inspired him.
en.firstFire: Look, that fire creature is causing damage to the city! We must put it out
en.tutorialUpstairsFire: Look, there are fires on top of that building! Go put them out!
en.3f841428: Finally! I’m home for real now! Yay!
en.a8def6c7: Amazing work rookie, you'll be an excelent firefighter!
en.ea88f101: Right! Foam is one of the things we will test
en.a6057132: Yes, the fire is essential for the experiment!
en.c9e6f998: Right! We will test how the water puts out the fire.
en.c78c9044: Yeah! We need the hose to spray the water and the foam!
en.e38f0053: Yes, we will use the box as fuel for the fire!
en.3ea16504: We are not sure which one is better...
en.6f414a17: We will have to use the scientific method to discover that, but we will talk about that later.
en.67c91483: Good thing everyone is out of town on vacation today.
en.125b6aaf: Oh no, it seems like you're out of foam, try using water now!
en.254a90d9: Oh no, it seems like you're out of water, try using foam now!
en.42185d27: Now you go there and put those fires out!
en.ad177de4: Oh no, it seems like you're out of water, try using foam now!
en.046a4b75: Touch and drag your screen to move.
en.3a60df11: Press the arrow keys to move.
en.a4e3c453: Hey, this is a new area, your water and foam hoses were recharged!
en.a03c1e4f: Hey, remember the hypothesis you chose and test it!
en.b36c5b79: You can change between water and foam at anytime by clicking on their icons.
en.cc2ebf67: Click on the material you observed to put out fire faster:
en.68ddb90b: Click on the stopwatch to start and observe the experiment!
en.cf39b0a6: Click on the boxes to start a fire.
en.fd00481d: That doesn't seem right, water seemed to be slower...
en.e2402e81: You're right, foam still seems faster! Our experiment is consistent even when we have more fire!
en.17b1cdb0: It will probably take a bit longer in general, but the results should remain consistent. Let's see!
en.3009a3cd: What would you consider were the results of this experiment?
en.3009a3cd_answer_0: Foam puts out fire faster than water
en.3009a3cd_answer_1: Water puts out fire faster than foam
en.3009a3cd_answer_2: Both water and foam puts out fire at the same speed
en.463db8c2: We can now analyze our experiment and figure out what it means! This is the fourth step of the scientific method.
en.d737e28f: That's right, you're a fast learner!
en.b2249b21: Now, let's see how that compares to our original hypothesis!
en.b65ea886: That's not quite right... Our experiment showed something different. Try again.
en.9d972954: Click on what you think it's needed for the experiment
en.731e406d: So foam is proven to be better than water to put out fires! This is our conclusion!
en.5a340adb: Our hypothesis is equal to the experiment result, which means we confirmed it!
en.c90b1f04: Our initial hypothesis was different than the experiment result, which means we learned something new!
en.4ce7d873: Oh yeah, as we thought, there are samples in the fire. Just put the fires out we will have all the samples to make our scientific experiment.
en.titleRateTheGame: Hey, don’t forget to rate the game!
en.1381b70f: A trial a single test of what happens to the variables during an experiment.
en.1520: one thousand five hundred and twenty
en.034e33a6: The number inside a period is the First Name, and the period is the last name. Repeat that for all periods
en.09ccfd8b: The position of each digit determines the digit's place value
en.74402076: Two billion, three hundred fifty million, one hundred twenty-five thousand, one
en.f84d677d: Each three decimal places make a period, like Billions, Millions, Thousands and Ones. You can see them separated in colors in the table below
en.16a81b46: They are separated by a comma, like in the number 1,000 for example. Remember this division so you can read big numbers correctly!
en.Result_Smaller: RESULT: Your number IS LESS than the other! Try Again
en.Result_Equal: RESULT: Your number IS EQUAL to the other! Try Again
en.Result_Bigger: RESULT: Your number IS GREATER than the other! Try Again
en.81059ea3: We use symbols to represent "Equal to", "Greater than" and "Less than"
en.af429b1c: Three thousand three hundred thirty three
en.9b6da512: One thousand four hundred ninety two
en.8e642b8c: One million one hundred thirty two thousand four hundred fifty
en.d7baecca: ninety nine thousand nine hundred ninety nine
en.f2939df7: Tip: Think First Name Last Name
en.79940be1: Oh no, I need to go that way to get back home!
en.52001e68: Yeah that's right! Now, drag me in the direction of the fish you think I can make the right comparison with the number I have!
en.317cbc25: Oh look at that, I have this symbol now instead of the words
en.0a6631a8: I think I remember what it means...but let's double check
en.4f57cc72: Alright! So I just have to remember that each symbol means one type of comparison! Let's go!
en.42ae0e73: Oh, some of these numbers are in written form!
en.9b315f99: Okay, so I need to remember both what each word is, and what period it is a part of!
en.2ea92a4b: I bet if I can compare correctly this number that I have with the ones of those fishes who are blocking my way, I could scare them off!
en.aba32a96: Let me just try to remember the basics...
en.NumberPeriod_TTS:
Each three decimal places make a period, like Billions, Millions, Thousands and Ones. You can see them separated in colors in the table below
They are separated by a comma, like in the number 1,000 for example. Remember this division so you can read big numbers correctly!
en.ComparisonSymbol_TTS: We use symbols to represent "Equal to", "Greater than" and "Less than".
en.WrittenForm_TTS:
The number inside a period is the First Name, and the period is the last name. Repeat that for all periods.
So in this example: Two billion, three hundred fifty million, one hundred twenty-five thousand, one
en.120800: One hundred twenty thousand eight hundred
en.350200: Three hundred fifty thousand two hundred
en.500000: Three hundred fifty thousand two hundred
en.650000: Three hundred fifty thousand two hundred
en.3245121: Three hundred fifty thousand two hundred
en.625000: Three hundred fifty thousand two hundred
en.323500: Three hundred twenty-three thousand five hundred
